title: Why AI Agents Need an Identity, Not Just Instructions
gist: As AI agents become autonomous and mediate millions of customer interactions, BCG argues companies must embed their purpose, values and culture into agents as operational logic, or risk 'scaling sameness' and eroding brand differentiation; the article frames a new translational discipline turning tacit culture into computable behavior.

themes.theme: Culture as competitive differentiation in AI agents

insights.claim: Two retailers using the same underlying AI model but different cultural grounding produce technically sound yet very different customer responses, and across millions of interactions those differences compound into divergent customer relationships.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/vladimir-lukic
insights.so_what: Model parity means differentiation shifts to how culture is encoded into agents; the moat is configuration and values, not the base model.
insights.quote: Two retailers deploy AI agents built on the same underlying model, configured to the same standards of helpfulness, accuracy, and compliance.

insights.claim: When agents are deployed without cultural grounding, organizations scale sameness, and if every organization relies on agents that behave the same way, differentiation disappears at the crossroads where customers decide whether to keep engaging.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/vladimir-lukic
insights.so_what: Commoditized agent behavior is a new competitive risk; brand erosion happens at the interaction level, not the marketing level.
insights.quote: When agents are deployed without cultural grounding, organizations scale sameness.
insights.locator: When agents are deployed without cultural grounding

insights.claim: Most AI agents today are built to optimize for performance metrics like speed, accuracy, efficiency and compliance, leaving them aligned to metrics rather than meaning and creating drift from organizational identity.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/vladimir-lukic
insights.so_what: The prevailing metric-driven agent design is a strategic blind spot; firms over-indexed on KPIs may be quietly eroding brand value.
insights.quote: They are aligned to metrics, not to meaning.

insights.claim: Embedding identity means turning corporate purpose into the system's foundational 'why', values into how agents make tradeoffs when goals conflict, and behaviors into interaction protocols for how agents communicate, escalate and respond emotionally.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/vladimir-lukic
insights.so_what: Provides a concrete three-layer framework (purpose, values, behaviors) for operationalizing brand into agents; a checklist for evaluating enterprise AI deployments.
insights.quote: Corporate values become how agents make decisions and manage tradeoffs.
insights.locator: If organizations want AI systems to reflect their identity

insights.claim: Making culture computable is fundamentally the challenge of making tacit knowledge explicit, since values like 'act with integrity' or 'put the customer first' are beliefs shaped by context and precedent, not executable instructions.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/vladimir-lukic
insights.so_what: The hard work is curatorial and cultural, not technical; vendors selling pure tech solutions miss the core challenge.
insights.quote: The challenge of making culture computable is, at its core, the challenge of making tacit knowledge explicit.

insights.claim: The fastest approach to encoding identity is prompt and system architecture, which structures agent inputs to include explicit direction on what to optimize for, what to refuse and how to reason through ambiguity, letting organizations see and adjust guidance over time.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/vladimir-lukic
insights.so_what: Prompt-and-system architecture is the practical entry point for enterprises encoding brand into agents; lowest cost, fastest iteration.
insights.quote: This is the fastest approach to deploy because organizations can more easily see how the AI is being guided and adjust its behavior over time
insights.locator: The first is prompt and system architecture.

insights.claim: A deeper approach is fine-tuning and value-weighted training, which adjusts models using selected examples (including call-center transcripts, executive interviews and internal communications) showing how the organization's best people reasoned, what tone they used and which exceptions they made.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/vladimir-lukic
insights.so_what: Fine-tuning on proprietary interaction data turns a company's behavioral history into a defensible asset; data exhaust becomes a cultural moat.
insights.quote: A luxury retailer, for example, might use hundreds of scenarios to demonstrate how its best advisors handled difficult situations
insights.locator: A second possibility, and one that provides deeper encoding

insights.claim: A third, still-experimental approach uses rule-based governance layers that evaluate whether agent responses reflect organizational values before delivery, functioning as a real-time cultural audit and guardrail to prevent drift.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/vladimir-lukic
insights.so_what: Real-time value-auditing layers are an emerging product category in enterprise AI governance; early-stage but watch for tooling vendors here.
insights.quote: In effect, they function as a real-time cultural audit and guardrail.

themes.theme: Organizational implications and the human bottleneck

insights.claim: Culture that cannot be described with enough specificity to teach to a human will not survive translation into a system, making the prerequisite work defining what values actually mean under pressure and at the edges.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/vladimir-lukic
insights.so_what: Firms with vague or implicit cultures are structurally disadvantaged in the agent era; clarity of values becomes an operational asset.
insights.quote: Culture that cannot be described with enough specificity to teach to a human will not survive translation into a system.

insights.claim: Responsibility for managing AI agents remains unclear in most organizations, spanning brand leaders, technology teams, operations, sales and strategy, raising unresolved questions about how to measure cultural drift across millions of interactions.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/vladimir-lukic
insights.so_what: Unsettled ownership of agent governance is an organizational gap; the firms that resolve it gain a coordination advantage.
insights.quote: Today, in most organizations, the responsibility remains unclear.
